examples.title: System Call - Process Creation (fork)
examples.description: Demonstrates how the fork() system call creates a new process in UNIX-like systems
examples.code:
FUNCTION fork() RETURNS integer
BEGIN
  // This is a pseudocode representation
  // Actual fork involves kernel trap
  
  SAVE current process context
  SET mode = KERNEL_MODE
  
  // Kernel creates process control block
  new_PCB = ALLOCATE process control block
  new_PCB.parent = current_process
  new_PCB.state = READY
  new_PCB.program_counter = current_PC
  
  // Copy parent's address space to child
  COPY_MEMORY(parent_address_space, child_address_space)
  
  // Return twice - once to parent, once to child
  IF current_process is parent THEN
    RETURN child_PID to parent
  ELSE
    RETURN 0 to child
  ENDIF
END
examples.explanation: The fork() system call creates an exact copy of the calling process. Both parent and child continue execution from the same point, but the return value differs - parent gets child's PID, child gets 0. This demonstrates kernel-mode process creation.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) - new process requires separate memory allocation

examples.title: System Call - File Open Operation
examples.description: Shows the sequence of operations when opening a file through system call interface
examples.code:
FUNCTION open(filename, flags, mode) RETURNS file_descriptor
BEGIN
  // Step 1: User library prepares request
  SET request_params = {filename, flags, mode}
  
  // Step 2: Trigger software interrupt to enter kernel
  TRAP_TO_KERNEL(SYS_open, request_params)
  
  // Step 3: Kernel execution (privileged mode)
  SET current_process = GET_CURRENT_PROCESS()
  
  // Validate permissions
  IF NOT CHECK_PERMISSION(filename, current_process) THEN
    RETURN -1 (error: EACCES)
  ENDIF
  
  // Locate or create file entry
  inode = GET_INODE(filename)
  IF inode NOT EXISTS THEN
    IF flags INCLUDE O_CREAT THEN
      inode = CREATE_NEW_INODE(filename, mode)
    ELSE
      RETURN -1 (error: ENOENT)
    ENDIF
  ENDIF
  
  // Allocate file descriptor in process table
  fd = ALLOCATE_FILE_DESCRIPTOR(current_process)
  current_process.file_table[fd] = inode
  
  // Step 4: Return to user mode
  RETURN fd
END
examples.explanation: The open() system call demonstrates kernel-mediated file access. It validates permissions, locates or creates file metadata, and returns a file descriptor for subsequent read/write operations. All privileged operations happen in kernel mode.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log n) for directory lookup + O(1) for descriptor allocation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for file descriptor table entry

examples.title: Mode Switching - User to Kernel Transition
examples.description: Demonstrates how CPU switches from user mode to kernel mode using system calls
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the complete flow of a system call: user program calls library function → library triggers trap → CPU switches to kernel mode → kernel validates and executes request → returns to user mode. This ensures protected access to hardware resources.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for mode switch + O(n) for actual operation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for state save/restore + O(n) for operation buffers

examples.title: Loadable Kernel Module (LKM) Structure
examples.description: Demonstrates the structure of dynamically loadable kernel modules
examples.explanation: Loadable Kernel Modules allow adding functionality to Linux kernel at runtime without rebooting. The module_init() function runs when loading, module_exit() runs when unloading. This demonstrates modular OS design where drivers can be dynamically added/removed.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for device memory allocation, where n is device buffer size
